frames.name: Legal Victory Frame
frames.description: Presents the story as a vindication of constitutional principles and rule of law, emphasizing the law firms' successful resistance to unconstitutional orders
frames.emphasis: Constitutional protections, judicial rulings declaring orders unconstitutional, and firms standing up for principles

frames.name: Administrative Chaos Frame
frames.description: Focuses on the DOJ's contradictory actions and lack of explanation for the reversal, highlighting governmental dysfunction
frames.emphasis: The 'stunning reversal,' 'abrupt' change, and absence of explanation from the administration

frames.name: David vs. Goliath Frame
frames.description: Portrays the law firms as principled defenders against presidential intimidation, contrasting them with firms that 'capitulated'
frames.emphasis: Firms that 'stood up to Trump' versus those showing 'capitalistic cowardice'

outlierPerspectives: The Guardian's framing of other law firms' settlements as 'capitalistic cowardice' - a moral judgment not echoed by other outlets
Axios's business-focused angle on Paul Weiss's cost-benefit analysis for their $40 million settlement deal
missingContext: Specific details about what the executive orders actually mandated or prohibited
Timeline of when the original executive orders were issued
Legal precedents for presidential actions against law firms
Potential consequences the targeted firms faced beyond loss of security clearances and building access
Details about the $1 billion in pro bono work commitments from settling firms
Explanation of why the DOJ reversed course within 24 hours
